title: Hot biexcitons driven by extreme optical confinement
authors: Xinyi Wang, Kaushik Kudtarkar, Wenjing Wu, Yunjo Jeong, Yuxuan Cosmi Lin, Xiaofeng Qian, Junichiro Kono, Shengxi Huang, Shoufeng Lan
abstract: A powerful means to understanding condensed matter that possesses a multi-constituent, non-isolated, and complex nature, with a preeminent example being two-dimensional (2D) materials, is studying many-body interactions. However, experimentally observing high-order many-body interactions is a daunting task due to its heavy reliance on the abundance of low-order complexes. Here, we report the observation of four-body hot biexcitons in an energetically unfavorable bilayer of tungsten disulfide (WS2) through creating extreme optical confinement. Specifically, we integrate a non-radiative bound state in the continuum (BIC) into a photonic crystal (PhC) defect cavity, forming a quasi-three-dimensional (q-3D) but open confinement for photons at the driving frequency. The extremely confined photons in both reciprocal and physical spaces then excite inherently unproductive two-body hot excitons situated slightly above the indirect bandgap so efficiently that they form overwhelmed higher-order four-body hot biexcitons. Distinctively, these hot biexcitons exhibit substantial valley polarization and coherence at room temperature, which we attribute to the topological nature of BICs and the associated q-3D confinement with an orbital angular momentum. Besides achieving room-temperature biexcitons, the q-3D confinement could be valuable for higher-order interactions, such as triexcitons, and many other many-body phenomena, including Bose-Einstein condensation.
summary: The researchers successfully observed four-body hot biexcitons in bilayer WS2 by using a photonic crystal cavity to create extreme optical confinement. This method leverages the topological properties of bound states in the continuum to achieve high-order many-body interactions at room temperature. The technique provides a potential pathway for studying even more complex phenomena like triexcitons or Bose-Einstein condensation.
main_problem: The difficulty of experimentally observing high-order many-body interactions, such as four-body complexes, in 2D materials due to the reliance on low-order complexes.
main_result: Observation of four-body hot biexcitons in a bilayer of tungsten disulfide (WS2) at room temperature using extreme optical confinement.
method: Integration of a non-radiative bound state in the continuum (BIC) into a photonic crystal (PhC) defect cavity to create quasi-3D optical confinement.
model_or_system: A bilayer of tungsten disulfide (WS2) placed within a photonic crystal defect cavity that utilizes BICs to achieve extreme confinement in both reciprocal and physical space.
key_observables: Valley polarization and coherence of the hot biexcitons.
important_parameters: Room temperature; quasi-three-dimensional (q-3D) confinement; orbital angular momentum.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of many-body interactions in 2D materials, describes the implementation of a BIC-integrated photonic crystal cavity, reports the observation of four-body hot biexcitons, discusses the role of topological BICs in valley polarization, and suggests future applications for higher-order interactions.
why_interesting: The use of topological BICs to engineer extreme light-matter interaction and drive the formation of high-order many-body complexes (four-body biexcitons) at room temperature is highly relevant to quantum optics and many-body dynamics.
